DA is also a nice way to upload the first few pages and get some decent feedback from your viewers before taking things to a publisher and having THEM review it a million and one times.

You're strips are hilarious. I loved Mr. Nader and Jennifer. The strip with Mr. Nader with the last panel saying "He's do dense to be guilty" or something like that. I can't remember. Either way I loved them haha

Oh yea, and the law firm run by Gort from "The Day the Earth Stood Still". That reminds me, did you see that super-crappy remake of that movie? They had Gort turn into friggin nanomachines! Gort! The inspiration for Scott Summers, Cylon Centurions, and the Knight Two Thousand. Gort, the imposing silver standard against which all killer robots are judged, reduced to a cloud of stoopid nanomachines. I am so glad that movie flopped. There should be a rule, a LAW in Hollywood that states: If you get to a point in your film making career, where you believe a Giant Silver Robot with Laser Beam Eyes isn't enough to hold an audience's interest on its own merits, that you should be ejected from the film industry on the spot! There just aren't enough slaps to go around. End rant. And thanks for remembering.
This certainly takee me back man. I think I'd be interested to see what this duo has to say about our current culture. You know, through guns and violence and such.
Don't forget swords. They'd use swords too. We have to keep in mind their full vocabulary when making statements.
Too True.
soo does he has his own version of Launchpad? (sidekick to Darkwing Duck and pilot for scrooge mcduck)

Sorry no well-meaning, incompetent aviators here. Being a veteran of the New Zealand conflict, Daniel Dukke is an expert fighter ace among his other copious attributes. When the need arises to get around the globe fast, he pilots his privately owned Lockheed YF-12 interceptor (Imagine an SR-71 with weapons.) himself. For the most part Deeds does his own driving and piloting, in spite of the fact that Ferdinand openly covets the driver's seat of Mr. Dukke's insanely cool sports car. Knowing this, Daniel tends to use this fact as a means of behavior modification. "Leave a thug alive for questioning, and you get to drive the car." Sadly, Ferdinand rarely gets to drive the car.
Heh I haven't seen this yet.. Looking nice! Also hows things going on your end?

That's amusing, since the drawing and the characters depicted therein are positively ancient. I should send you the old graphic novel I made with them, although I should warn you, while some of the humor still holds up, I was learning how to draw as I produced it. On the other thing, progress is slower than I'd hoped. My friends tell my I'm being too much of a perfectionist and they're probably right. I think I'll start posting the character design drawings and such here in the gallery to whet people's appetites. Or a least let them know I'm alive. How are things going on your end? Talk to Ron or Jason at all?

Yeah send away, and if theirs no Publishing rights to any other group, I'll be more then pay to publish this. As for Being a Perfectionist, well don't feel bad I'm getting this problem with J.ro as well. XD Heh he. I have it so bad that sometimes I never even START a picture because I know I'll just make my self unhappy with what I've drawn. However at least you have real talent and skill. The thing with being a perfectionist is just learning the right time to let issues go. That not everyone will see what you see, and the ones that do... Well if you did even a decent job, and not half assed it, you then still did a damn good job because you actuly put effort in to makeing it not half assed. And you and I know alot of art that does look half assed still gets through unfortunetly in comics and those guys still get their check at the end of the month. So try to find a middle ground, try to find an area that you'll "Accept" the art and let it go eventhough you'll be fustrating over it in your head.
Now THIS brings back Memories. I think I still have mine from 95.
I guess you must be from the European branch of the family, cuz y'all spell your name funny It's a mighty nice drawing, though.
I wear a goatee, just so you know. I'm the evil twin!!!
I didn't know dukkes could grow beards. Sounds... odd. So, how often do you have to kill someone for pronouncing your name "duckie"?
Never, they always call me "Duke", totally ignoring the fact that my favorite character is Destro.
So, you're prefer to be called the Destro of death?
Well that pun backfired.
happens to the best of us
Oh crap, it's you. I still have that comic book you drew the bimbunnies in for me so long ago.
Thanks for writing in, it's good to be remembered.
Supremely iconic with an amazing old-school coloring job. I am blown away.
I'm still surprised I never blew up my house with all those marker fumes.
This is unbelievably radical.
Thanks!!

title: Bat-Sketch
description: I am Batman! Gaze upon my magnificent chin and know the touch of dread! Just a sketch.

title: Chibi Batgirl Color
description: Color of chibified Batgirl picture. Edit: Just to pre-empt anyone accusations of my coloring her hair wrong, I meant this to be the new Stephanie Brown version.

comments.body: she looks so happy, falling to her death like that haha
Oh you.

Heeey, you should do commissions! I always loved your style and I'd love to see my girl as one of your pin ups Come on, you know you waaaana! (Oddly, I think I've used that line with you before? o.O )
Thank you for saying so. So which one is your girl? Dazzler?
You're welcome, and nope. Bandeau is mine, the one with the wings [link]
Does she have any gadgets?
Mmm she has a crystal sword, that's about as gadgety as she gets I'm not good with drawing technology, so I tend to stick with the whole "grunt, ugh ugh, stab" aspect of character defense.
Looks really good. Only confusion I've got is, isn't she a redhead?

In the comics, the original red headed Batgirl was shot and paralyzed by the Joker. [link] (Which should have been the straw that finally made Bats kill the pasty bastard, but that's just my opinion. ) Since that time, Babs has taken on the new identity of the handy-capable Oracle [link] , who provides intelligence and information to the super-hero community, providing a long needed counterpoint to data-hacking villains like The Calculator. Barbara has also trained two young women to succeed her in the mantle of Batgirl, an Asian Girl, Cassandra Cain, [link] and most recently, the blonde daughter of the super-villainous Trickster, Stephanie Brown. [link] In the case of Barbara Gordon, while I miss her as the original Batgirl I actually admire DC Comics' courageous decision not to magically overturn her paraplegia. In her new role as Oracle, and the mentor of her successors, she has become a character who despite injury, is never taken out of the fight. [link]
I like the idea of the new Batgirl. Will have to get used to her being blonde....but we blondes do it better anyway
Yes but her mentor is still a red head.
Yeah, but Barbara Gordon is an ok redhead. It's rare but happens.

title: Chibi Super Girl Color
description: A quick coloring of the chibified Supergirl picture. Hope you like what you see.

comments.body: the cuteness is overwhelming...and then I saw the Daily Planet's eyes and died.
Gotta remind myself to tray and do a whole city like this.
any chance of you doing Powergirl anytime soon? your coloring is as slick as ever.
Powergirl sounds like a nice idea.
since im reading a few powergirl comic these day, i thought she would be a good try once in a while.
Adorable!
Thanks! Guh. I wish the show was still fun.

title: Connie and Kwewsah
description: I don't remember if I said anything about adding Star Trek characters to my long-procrastinated Chiroine's series, but since the new, non-sucky movie came out, I've seen so much Chibi/Cutesy/Sd/Etc... Star Trek art that I felt moved to sketch up some li'l ships. Here is a sketch of a Constitution Class vessel, along the lines of the new movie's design. I couldn't quite make out what the new Klingon vessels looked like during their all too brief appearance, so I decided to go with the "tried-and-true" D-7 cruiser, or to go with the "cutesified" theme "kwewsah" design. I'm sure any and all true Klingons would surely disembowel me for what I've done here. Star Trek, and all related characters and concepts are © and ™ Paramount Studios.

comments.body: Chibi-Starships? Very impressive work mate
Over that last few weeks, I've seen every other aspect of Star Trek get chibified,[link] so it seemed like a natural progression to me.
lol aha. Well you did a great job with them
You're alive! <3
Yea man. I'm like a horror movie villain.
*cues the cathedral organ music*
Don't forget the ominous sounding choir.
Oh, I'm sorry, I was channeling Christopher Lee's "Hammer Horror" movie years.
Ha , that I never thought . chibi star trek . well done .
Coming from you that is high praise indeed. Thank you.

title: Thundercats Nya WIP
description: A work in progress picture for the next Chiroine. I'm liking the chibified design for the tank, but I don't know if it's quite the POV I want. I'll know for sure when I work in Chetara.(Funny way of working, I know, but I really felt like drawing the tank first.) Let me know what folks think.

title: Missing Missiles
description: More with the dog fight comic. Here I wanted to portray a jet threading its way through one of those ludicrously intense swarms of impossibly maneuverable, random trajectory missiles you see in every anime ever filmed! Seriously, I think I saw one in Peach Girl the other day.

comments.body: well the proportion on the nose of the fighter is off a bit it should be straight rather than pointing diagonally downwards.everything else is perfect.
Huh. It's always amazing what you don't see once you get used to looking at a drawing. Good observation.

yeah i hate looking at something a month later and noticing that type o shit.makes me feel weird but even the great jimlee said he takes breaks in between to double check proportions. you dev watching him too?
But I can't seem to find the hidden Budweiser can in the swarm...
Very very impressive work ^^ I like the little addition you've made to the F-15's airframe

Hmmm. Er. Ah.. how do I put this? Actually, it's supposed to be an Su-37. [link] Looking at the picture, I can see that was spot on about how I misinterpreted the jet's nose, but I didn't think I was that far off-model. Oh well. That's why pencils come with erasers.

Oh I'm sorry, I guess it was a perspective thing for me. And given the plans have similar profiles and I was kinda expecting the Eagle I made that mistake. Sorry about that, I think it's easy enough to do really. They're very similar aircraft when all's said and done. I mean now you've shown me the pic I can see that yes that is what it's meant to be. Sorry about that. Love the way you've got it right in the middle of all the missiles without being hit... well not yet anyway.
NP, dewd. I was just worried I was going to have to redraw the whole thing.
Only thing that might need work is the nose, but now I know what you meant to draw (given the similarity between aircraft, roles and all) it is obvious as a Su-37

Awesome!!! But maybe in order to improve on that "threading its way through effect" you might wanna put the plan on a more of an angle, add sum flashy speed lines, and have the wings or body bend a bit. Like it looks really good, i can see you are really clinical with drawing. It just seems like the pilot is reading the newspaper while misconfigured rockets fly around him. Nice work overall!

That's funny, 'cause I drew the speed lines on a separate piece of paper to photoshop in later. I was beginning to lose track of things with all the missiles. (So much so, I forgot to throw in the hidden beer can.) I figured that adding the speed lines to the original pencils would give me vertigo. While there are some details I need to fix on the plane, I'm going to opt out of "squash n' stretching" it in this picture. I may add motion blur to a few elements, like the foreground missiles, but that can wait until I ink and color everything. We'll see. Considering how bad ass the pilot's supposed to be here, I'm reserving the option to actually have her reading a newspaper during all this.
sorry, but which anime had the hidden beer can? i heard about it on the news in my youth, but they never mentioned the title.
It's during one of the space battles in Project A-KO. Heh, that anime was made in 1986, and the missile thing was played out and spoof-worthy back then!

That is a BUTTLOAD of missiles! Is that one plane firing all of them, and if not, how has it not been blown to smithereens? I'm not by any means an airplane buff, but the plane is fantastically well done. ...Actually, this sorta reminds me of this thing the U.S. military developed with a big, computerized frame that spins off seven guided bombs which in turn spin off several more bombs which themselves burst into clusters of bomblets. By the time it gets to the ground there's enough explosives to take out a tank division. Of course, each explosion is to the tune of 3 million dollars (or was it 30 mil?), but that hasn't stopped them from using it.

Nope the plane is in fact dodging all the missiles. It's a long established custom that missiles never hit anything in anime, they just swarm about getting ever so close to the heroes craft in order to better demonstrate what a complete and utter bad ass he or she is. I think I've seen those cluster bomby killamajigs you mentioned. It's awesome seeing them all fan out. It'll be interesting to see how they get protrayed in anime.

Hey the Missile spam is just awesome Except in online servers. Everytime I'm flying my precious MiG-35 some noob starts flying some big missile carrier aircraft and starts spamming the skies with them. But the adredile rush is just the win This picture is pretty awsome. Love the details on the Su-33/Su-35.

"Missile Spam?" There's that phrase again. Ah, I see. I didn't realize there was a term for those crazy missile fusillades in anime. Considering they were old hat as far back as 1986, I really should have been surprised if there wasn't one by now. That's going to save me a lot of typing in the future. However, I really do like the word "fusillade" Thanks for the compliments on the jet. It's still got some tweaking before I hit it all with the inks, but I'm glad the reactions been positive so far.

Well, I've come across another term (on the tvtropes wiki), although it's rather unwieldy. There's also an (incomplete) list of shows that do it, so I figure you may appreciate that. If not, I'm sorry for butting in. [link]
Hmmm, a Missile Spam Glossary. Thanks, This is SO coming in handy in the future.
Oh, trust me, there's so much more on that website than just that: I have lost many hours in a wiki-induced haze, wandering from link to link. Be careful!
You got me at least.
Alas; it's a wonderful site, but even they admit that it will ruin your life!
That's a good thing to happen to an artist
Anime Missiles are nothing but manueverable rockets. They rarely hit and usually it's only in Visual Range It's for the choregraphy i guess~

Yea, cartoon or movie battle has very little to do with the real thing. For instance, the chief advantage of the aircraft carrier is the ability to strike targets well beyond visual range. In fact, many of the legendary carrier battles of WWII took place without the opposing carriers ever coming within sight of each other. However, in a fictional show, like Battlestar Galactica, battlestars and basestars slug it out within artillery range of one another as each vehicle's fighter compliment mix it up while trying to dodge flack and fratricidal ordinance. Ridiculous, but it looks awesome.
oh yah, great illustration too~
Thank you very much.

This thing is awesome (and what you said about anime missiles is absolutely true). Don't forget the cliche teenage boy (or girl?) who instantly transforms into a brilliantly lethal ace pilot the moment he/she so much as touches a fighter jet/giant robot.

Yea I know. It would be fun once to do a comic in which some anime kid with a Zac Effron haircut stumbles upon a war machine "Unlike any he's ever seen before..." during the requisite alien invasion, and when he takes it out for a spin BÜME! is immediately blown into so many free-floating ionized particles. Actually, maybe he's not disintegrated, let's leave a barely recognizable corpse in the twisted remains of the mech, so some grizzled veteran can survey the aftermath and say. "Stupid kid? What he think he was going to do--beat battle hardened veteran without an ounce of training? What he should have done was run!" Then out of nowhere Horatio Cain will show up, don his cool shades and say, "Yea, every one knows how to do that. WIIAAAAAUUUUGGH! We Won't get fooled again... But I digress.
Brilliant! This one needs those vapors trailing the wing tips as the pilot spins to avoid collision.
Haw. Good observation. I gots ta make a note of that.
Is that a Sukhoi Su-37 Terminator?

Why yes, yes it is. This is a panel from a Gold Digger annual submission that my lazy ass never finished. It was to feature Skippy Von Richtoven showing off her preternatural flying skills. They idea was that she would be flying the only non-stealth jet in a training exercise against other Night Flight regulars. I figured that, with Skippy's unique heritage, the tri-winged Terminator would be the only appropriate ride for her--painted bright red of course.
I see Why not chose the Su-47 Berkut? I've seen that in the Ace Combat series of PS games

Interesting. While not a stealth fighter per se, it does have a reduced radar cross section. Plus, its reverse swept wing would give it extreme maneuverability, and there's also it's resemblance to the X-29 piloted by her beloved rival Kevin Koss. Excellent suggestion. However, the Su-37 just looks cooler and more menacing to my taste, and its more pronounced moveable horizontal tails gives it a much more prominent three-wing look, appropriate for the descendent of the Red Baron. But your idea has much merit.

Thnaks Also if I'm right what the US introduced on the F-22 Raptor the Soviets/Russians too adopted and improved for the Sukhois by not just letting move the exhaust of the engines horizontally but in every direction as well giving even extra manoeuvrability. Don't remember if this introduced in the Su-47 as well. And of course there is the new generation of Mig fighters as well the Mig-144 (or Mig 1.44?? )

Wait... wait... Skippy Von Richtoven? The unholy merger of Poe "Muthafuckin" Dameron and Mobius 1? THE greatest pilot to touch a stick in the Gold Digger universe? (Death Boid ain't nothing, and Ace... he might be an equal, the only reason why the Skippster is so obsessed.)

The one and only. This was a panel from a fan comic that I never got around to finishing. I mainly drew it, because, with its small canard, forward of its main wing, the Su-37 Terminator has been referred to as a triple wing aircraft. It seemed so obvious that the successor to the Von Richtoven family name should pilot the spiritual successor to the old Fokker Dr-1. In the fan strip, the balance of Night Flight, flying Y--23's, (Think Raptors, only not ugly.) spar against Skippy, writing her off, due to their stealth advantage. She pastes them all of course, her skill overcoming the advantages of numbers and technology enjoyed by her opponents. On the matter of Skippy, I recall reading an issue in which Penny converses with what is apparently Skippy's ghost. I desperately hope she wasn't killed off. I loved that character.
Penny is obsessed with being between Skippy and Penny. I highly doubt Skippy's dead... that's Penny fantasizing.

That's certainly a relief to here. BTW, your comments regarding this old picture have inspired me to fish out some of the other Skippy related artwork I had lying about. johnstaton.deviantart.com/art/... johnstaton.deviantart.com/art/... Enjoy.
Very nice.

With well placed flares, chaff, and a good sense of the stick, this could happen. Most of the time though you'd better hope whoever chose the ejection seat for your aircraft didn't go for the lowest bid...
Good luck with that. It IS a Russian built plane.
Looking at the liveries, I wager there was some "post market" upgrades to correct that unfortunate pedigree. After all, whoever is piloting that plane hasn't been touched...
Actually, I just remembered, little Miss Von Richthofen herself, was responsible for equipment procurement for Night Flight. It's very likely, she'd carry the best ejection seat.

Not that she'd ever need it... but it's nice to have on hand. Actually I think it would actually be fitting for her to actually hold her own, making everyone else go full winchester, but she ends up having to bail because of the one thing everyone has to worry about... fuel. Of course when she lands in the drink she has a cute duckie float. Not so sure how she made a milspec life preserver look like that... but cute tends to follow her everywhere.
Well I did give her bunny patterned upholstery on the head rest of her pilot's seat. I'm sure she could manage a rubber ducky life preserver.
Hehe, well that or a life vest that looks more like a pool toy style one... but is as rugged as a typical LPU. With cute bunny things on it... because why not.
Itano circus outmanuevered! That's how sort the aces from the fodder, men! Mad levels of detail here.
*Googles "Itano circus"* Wow! Now I finally have a name for this phenomenon. Thank you sir.

Gotta make sure all the fans are up to date. Itano himself had a big effect on anime development by the economical use of frame rate for various scenes, which allowed studios to save yen and include more of the eye-poping movement visuals he made famous.
Itano? That is a person? (Pardon my ignorance. You could say "Itano" is a non dairy desert topping, and I'd believe you at this point.) Tell me more anything about this individual.

Ichiro Itano is one of the mighty men of mecha anime, without whom we would still be watching giant robots waddle across the screen. As a kid, he emulated an action hero from a TV hero by volley-firing bottle rockets from impromptu launchers taped to his bicycle. Later, as a young anime animation worker, he achieved the status of key animator. As a late addition to Mobile Suit Gundam, he helped the visionary but expensive SF series with its budget issues, and did some great animation work for it. Sadly anime work is an on & off thing for people who are not stars, and Gundam took a while to become the Big Thing it is now. So by 1982 Itano was driving a delivery truck as a day job. Some other guys who had worked on Gundam were at Studio Nue, and they wanted him to be the animation director for this show they were working on. That show was SDF Macross, and Itano was able to quit his delivery job. Here is his work record, which features some mighty shows indeed! myanimelist.net/people/8268/Ic...

Thank you for the link, and the lesson. I appreciate learning about the people behind the works that I love. Every time greatness is touched upon in any human endeavor, it is because of the participation of certain individuals who possess the right vision, the right grasp of their surrounding circumstances, or the right cocktail of unique talent to take their profession to the next plateau of excellence. Every landmark achievement, whether it be a dynastic sports team, a successful car line, or an influential animated cartoon, have at their core these special people, and their story only enriches one's appreciation of their masterwork.

title: Cockpit Shots
description: Pencilled panels from a submission I am working on. My ambition is to capture the dynamics of a Top Gun style dog fight on a comic page. Hard to tell, I know since you can only see the controls here. One thing that will always mystify me is why the "Bad ass fighter pilot" never became a legitimate movie genre. I mean few things on earth are as inherently cool as fighter jets. And I'm sure the military would cooperate in the filming of any number of motion pictures that made them look like flying rock stars, and yet still no Top Gun II.* You really have to wonder. *OK, there was Iron Eagle II. But that's just not the same.

comments.body: Omg i so agree. I love dogfights, if you can capture that on paper then i would rank you higher than most manga-ka. I'm more of a fan of WWII and there's even less of those. Sweet

I dunno mate. I've seen some pretty sweet dog fights in manga. As a matter of fact, manga's pretty much the only place outside of Antarctic Press comic books that I've seen dogfights done competently. But I do appreciate the vote of confidence. I love WWII dog fights as well. I just wish they had more 3rd and 4rth gen fighter jets in them. (Ever see Final Countdown? [link] Best of both worlds. )
Nice panels! Did you use references from anything specific?

That's more or less the cockpit of an Su-37 Terminator. (The plane dodging missile in the next picture.) I was fortunate enough to find some detailed books on Amazon.com containing all sorts of useful information. It's so cool now since the Berlin Wall came down.
Schematics FTW. Thanks.

The only other movies I can think of that were based around fighter jets were the Hotshot movies and Stealth, neither of which were in the same league as Top Gun. The scenes with the Harriers in True Lies showed again how exciting jets can be, though.

And True Lies was 15 years ago!! It will forever puzzle me why movies aggrandizing jets are so few and far between. I honestly think Hollywood has no real idea what's actually cool. For instance, this past weekend, I saw the remake of The Day the Earth Stood Still. It was horrible. I could fill the screen with complains about this waste of film. For one thing they had Keanu Reeves play the super intelligent alien Klaatu, and award winning actor John Hamm as the impotent authority figure who stands in the control room giving pensive, disaster movie exposition. That's like hiring Rembrandt and Jackson Pollack and asking Pollack to paint your portrait, while Rembrandt paints your fence. The most poignant issue I have with the movie is the unforgivable misuse of Gort. They take one of science fiction's most classic, and visually influential robot's and replace him with a cloud of nanomachines, because obviously some Hollywood type thought that a giant silver robot with a cycloptic laser beam eye wasn't cool enough to hold an audience's attention on its own merit!! When you get to the point in your film making career that you think things like fighter jets and giant laser-eyed robots aren't cool enoght for you, you need to retire on the spot. There endeth the rant.
The closest I can think of are "Blue Thunder" (the original movie, not the TV show) and "Airwolf".

Helicopter pilots just aren't the same to me. Although now that you bring up the 1980's super-vehicle genre, I do recall Firefox- the badass Clint Eastwood movie, not the web browser. Blue Thunder was a badass movie, too. Awful television show, but a cool movie.
Funny thing about Firefox; we kinda have those now, but they're called AF-22's!
Yea but those are American. I thought you were going to mention the PAK FA's, since those are actually Russian.
Thanx for the link. Never knew Russia had their own!

title: HIND-Butt
description: I honestly don't know if anyone remembers at this late point, but a short while ago I submitted a comic page depicting a Mil Mi-24 Hind-D attack helicopter about to meet what looked like a bad fate. [link] I promised if I ever found the subsequent page, I would post it. Well here it is. Marine Girl headbutting a Hind. In retrospect, I wish I had spent a lot more time on the third panel. I am disappointed with myself that I opted for "flash lines" to depict the destruction, rather than a detailed drawing of the ruined husk of the doomed machine as it spends its final instants aloft before surrendering to the welcoming embrace of gravity. I may have to redraw this on principle.

comments.body: So you might say the Russians had their Hind End handed to them by a Marine....

title: 'Peake Performance
description: The story behind this aging marker drawing is easily as amusing as the image itself. Years ago, I was commissioned to do a drawing for a Star Trek fan club, the eponymous U.S.S. Chesapeake. They wanted, quite simply, for me to draw a ship for them. I didn't realize that the club at the time would have been more than satisfied had I simply put the name "U.S.S. Cheasapeake" on a drawing of Star Trek: The Next Generation's famed Galaxy Class vessel; so being the mecha-nut I am, I designed an entirely new vessel. I decided to add a third warp nacelle, (Please note that I did this drawing well before the episode "All Good Things..." aired.) integrated into the saucer section, so that it would not become a lumbering, bulky target should the vessel have to separate. Said Nacelle would add power to the weapons system, in particular the dedicated saucer dish particle beam, when the ship's sections are united. Keen eyes will also note quite a preponderance of torpedo tubes lining the ships hull. Anyway, when I presented this battle wagon to the club, they rejected it out of hand because it wasn't the opulent explorer's vessel that Picard rolled around in. Normally, I would have simply gone back to my drawing board, but, being gleefully fluent in treknobabble, I decided it would be fun, this time, to debate the benefits of my ideas. At that point, I addressed the club and defended my concept like defense contractor fighting to keep a plant open. I pointed out that, in Next Generation, Starfleet's rules of engagement staunchly dictated that commander's were never to fire unless fired upon. Given that fact, and the situation that their two greatest territorial rivals: The Romulans and The Klingons boasted a prodigious first strike capability as well as an aggressive fleet disposition; it was certain that Federation starships would be called upon to respond to initial aggression. To carry out such a doctrine under those circumstances, Starfleet would need a second strike capable front line vessel, and quite frankly, the Galaxy Class simply wasn't it. I went on to enumerate the Next Generation Episodes1 in which the Enterprise 1701-D was incapacitated by obsolete, underpowered, sometimes ancient vessels whose only tactical advantage was that of first strike. TheFederation clearly needed a vessel that could absorb an attack from a war-surplus Bird-of-Prey piloted by a Ferenghi skeleton crew and respond in such a decisive manner as to deter future incursions from ambitious would-be aggressors. Just to drive the point home, I also mentioned that of the six total Galaxy-Class vessels, christened at that point in the 'Trek timeline,2 (Which were supposed to defend the Federation from the Romulan Star Empire which boasted at least 12 separate comparable D'Diridex-Class cruisers.) three were lost during deployment. (Four, if you count the Enterprise-D itself, during "Generations". And the Galaxy was just a test-bed ship!) I actually ended up selling the club on the idea of the ship. If I was a fictional Federation defense company, I would have made a mint contracting that gun-laden behemoth to Star Fleet. Well I would've, if there was money in the Star Trek future. 1.) Just to assuage the fears of the countless, annoying fuddy-duddies, who feel I'm going way too deep in my analysis of fictional outer space fleet dispositions and tactics--"Yes, I am aware this is all just a T.V. show!" so please feel free to bugger off. 2.) Edit: I had previously made what I considered to be a complete list of the names of the Big E's sister vessels, but I had included the USS Melborne, which a keen-eyed observer pointed out was an Excelsior-Class vessel. For the sake of accuracy and brevity, I felt it was better to omit the listing.

comments.body: *jaw drop.....

*rolls jaw backup How did you do this?! I mean, what was the size of the drawing on paper for the details on the ship? I assume the background was stripped from some image search of Nasa data. But wow, that is something on the details. Ever do official ST artwork before? This is a little too good.

I'm almost embarrassed to admit I got the background by dipping an old toothbrush in water soluble, white Goache paint and flicking the bristles with my thumb over a sheet of black construction paper. The drawing was 11" x 17" lineart done with Sakura Pigma pens, colored with Berol Prisma Color markers, color xeroxed, cut-out and rubber-cemented on to the construction paper. I used a little more Goache to emphasize some of the ship's numerous windows. I have to admit, Once I scanned the picture into the computer, I added a little Photoshop glow, around the Nacelles, and Bussard Collectors. (A cheat, I know, but I couldn't help myself.)

Wow! Thanks for the details on that. Interesting way of making a star map. Add a bit of a nebula near the mass of stars to really sell it. I seriously cannot tell the difference! Who would care if you cheated on the collectors?

Here is a good question that has been on my mind. I assume you have had contact with a bunch of other artists over your years. This technique used to create a star background on your image, do others use it?

Funny thing that. Back when I did this, it was actually all the rage to dip a toothbrush in ink and use it to create blood spatters. (I believe it was Jae Lee who popularized the technique.) I just reversed things by using gouache on black paper.

Wowsers! O.O This is truly, absolutely, amazing! This is an excellent candidate to fill that role the federation has no representative of: a Battleship. Sure this peaceful exploration stuff is all well and good but their galaxy is filled by, at one time or another, aggressive species. It makes sense to have a battleship class ship, even if you only have a few, instead of relying on what are basically exploration ships. Cause they're proven repeatedly inadequate for combat with ships of a similar scale from other races (ie a Galaxy class vs Romulan Warbird... no prises for guessing who should win that), as they're not armed well enough or defended well enough. ...But even then the Star Trek galaxy's races never really seemed to build a true battleship class starship, within canon, until the Scimitar in Nemesis. Now I'd pay money to watch your Chesapeake take on the Scimitar Anyway, stunning drawing. The commissioner sure as heck got his moneys's worth! Thanks for sharing
Well if the Chesapeake rammed the Scimitar, she'd immediately board the latter! It's actually naval doctrine to board a rammed enemy vessel. What a wasted opportunity.
Very true. Although, again, the Sovereign class (as impressive as it is) isn't a Warship and Picard doesn't really think that way, would have made much more sense though *nods*.

I love the philosophical tightrope the Federation always dances across. They always say their vessels are ships of peace, yet an average starship alone can lay waste to an entire nuclear-age civilization without breaking a sweat and they alone are called upon to defend the Federation in times of war. I think that the Federation would better served by having a seperate corps of diplomatic vessels, and a fleet of dedicated warships to patrol its borders. But then we couldn't have "West Wing" style political intrigue episodes one week, and "Red October" style adventure episodes the next, while paying for only one set and set of actors. Damn realities of television production.

LOL yeah I know what you mean, the original Enterprise could lay waste to the Earth right now. Obviously they decided to go that way cause it lets them have more potential plots within a series, instead of same old same old. But yeah logically it makes way more sense that you'd have exploration ships, arguably like the Galaxy Class (cause they'd have to be able to defend themselves in case a planet they discover has hostile inhabitants), then diplomatic ships and finally true warships. Horses for courses and all that. Rather then some compromise ship that can kinda fill all roles but will never excel at any of them.

Hmmm, in a sad way, it's sort of realistic. Think about the fact that whenever someone comes up with a decent air-superiority fighter, some pentagon numb-nutz tacks an "A" on it's designation and tries to make it do double duty as a bomber. F/A-18 anyone? I guess it's not just televison after all.

Yep, that's the way it goes. I think the only decent 'compromise' plane they've come up with is the ground attack capable variant of the F-15 Eagle. *Cringes* Yeah, rotten little plastic bug. What was wrong with trying to make a modern day equivalent of the F-14 (in terms of armament it can carry and overall range)? Have a particular dislike of the things cause they're what Australia operates as its front-line war aircraft... A role it is not suited for at all. And will be until the Lightning II comes on stream, since the Raptor won't be exported. We should just get a whole bunch of UCAVS, it'd work much better. Er, anyway yeah it's not just a TV convention to try for a vehicle that can be used in a variety of ways but not excel at any.
Pronoun trouble. Which craft is Australia's front line fighter?

Sorry my bad, currently it's the F/A-18 Hornet (the standard one... *shudders*) They're looking to replace it with the Lightning II, when that one comes online. Despite the delay issues with it, and despite some claims that it's not the right plane for us. Apparently the Raptor would meet our requirements much better, but the US won't export it. Although really I think our best opinion would be to buy a whole heap of F-15 Eagles. They'd do for what we need, and they've got the range, not sure about the range on the Raptor or Lightning II.
That, and the Eagle looks cooler. For all their advancement, stealth planes all look like glorified paper airplanes to me. All of them.

LOL Yeah there certainly is that! And where did individualism go? It's hard to distinguish between the Lightning II and the Raptor. The Eagle's faster then either of the others, of which the Lightning II is way slower (the other two can hit Mach 2, but the Lightning II can't). And The Eagle has a better range (by a least 1000miles). Couldn't find anything on the Lightning II's ceiling but the Raptor and Eagle have the same one. So they can be as stealthy as they like, given our distance issues I'll take the proven fast long range Air Superiority Fighter.

Also, considering the Eagle's 100+ -to-0 kill ratio, one almost questions the need for an new and heinously overpriced airframe. Why not build new F-15's and supply them with the Raptor's vaunted supercruise engines?

Particularly when you say the F-15 has a better overall range, and has reigned virtually unchallenged as the supreme Air Superiority Fighter in the world throughout it's service life (which'll run til 2025... that says something about it). Yeah that could well work ^^
The detail is just amazing.
Thanks.

Wow that's a lot of detail. Ok, I'm not exactly up on the Star Trek universe, but unless "torpedoes" are purely energy weapons I gotta ask: Where on earth does she make the magazine space to feed all those in an extended engagement? Very cool though.

I appreciate you allowing for the dignified "out" of torpedoes being energy weapons, but you know as well as I do that dormant torpedoes take the form of those funky, roughly 3 x 1.5 x 1 meter black lozenges that do double duty as coffins when the battle is over. In all honesty, you'd probably have to have another whole starship to carry enough lozenges to supply all the torpedo tubes on the "Peake" for any appreciable firing time. If I were to revisit this design today, I'd have a lot fewer tubes, stationed in places to supply the greatest range of defensive coverage. Plus I'd put a great deal less windows on the thing. It is a battleship after all.

Ah right, guess the fleet train's gonna have it's work cut out for it then... or the Chesapeake (on the assumption she's the lead ship) takes on more a strike role... which I assume from one of the earlier conversations on here is against the Federation's standard ROI. I was just wondering 'cause my first sci-fi love is the Honor Harrington universe where "torpedoes" are energy based, short range/high rate of fire (irespective to missiles) mounts... but where physical missiles can weigh in at over 100 tonnes and magazine capacity is a major limiting design factor (at least on smaller ships, for Wallers it's more keeping drive seperation). Yeah, windows are a nasty armor chink... besides: the people inside should be too busy not getting the ship blown out of space to look outside

Yours is probably a good system to go with. In my comments on another picture, I lamented how sci-fi universes establish rules to govern matters such as these and then liberally ignore them when they become inconvenient. Star Trek is, of course, no stranger to this phenomenon. Take for example Voyager a show whose entire premise rests on the fact that the wayward USS Voyager can never resupply or rearm. In the first season they make a point to establish that they have a mere 36 precious torpedoes, but by season six, there were episodes were they fired 50 or more. Actually, now that I think of it, maybe I should just be like a Voyager writer and say the Peake can fire continuous torpedo fusillades from all its tubes for umpteen hours due to the marvel of its core redundant, double-talk framistat generators. There, that fixes everything.

Wow, just read my previous post, so many errors, should not have written that just before going to bed... Yeah, I remember reading that one. Having grounded rules does make it easier for fans to work within the universe. That's probably one of the reasons I like Harrington, there is a ludicrous amount of literature on the tech, tactics, strategies, politics and so on of the universe, most of it written by the author or by fans who have trawled through the books to find it. Fun reading for the sci-fi technogeek ---> [link] "Framisat generator"... like teleporting torpedoes directly into tubes from a fleet collier?

Fleet Collier? Hmmm, you know Star Trek never addresses fleet trains and other support logistics for any of the grandiousely dispersed fleets it depicts. They pretend that every ship has nigh-unlimited range, but the standard mode of transportation of the Star Trek universe depends upon using Dilithium* to control the energy released by the mutual annihilation of Deuterium and Anti-Hydrogen. Annihilation. As in, once the fuel is converted to energy and used, there isn't even exhaust remaining as a byproduct. Star Ships should need to refuel frequently. There should be some form of fuel train system to deliver matter and antimatter to the hungry nacelles of all these ships soaring around the cosmos. These trains would be interesting as well, because the power who best protects their support network would be best able to hold galactic territory. But even beyond that. If one of these Collier Vessels can beam weapons into the magazines of starships, why not likewise beam live ordinance into enemy vessels? There's a lot of fun one can have with the inherent advantages and limitations of Star Trek technology that is never addressed. *Contrary to public perception, Dilithium is not actually a fuel source. It is coveted in the Star Trek Universe because it is the only substance that can focus and control a matter/antimatter reaction. The source of this general misconception is a line spoken by Scotty in the TOS episode Mudd's Women:"But it's frustrating. Almost a million gross tons of vessel depending on a hunk of crystal the size of my fist. " Most casual viewers look upon this line as an indication that dilithium is a futuristic miracle fuel, neglecting Spock's subsequent line:"And that crystal won't hold up, not pulling all our power through it." "Though" not "From." Dilithium is not fuel.

Beaming aboard live ordinace... a-la Stargate Atlantis? I guess it's a matter of weighing up the ranges of the beaming system and the ordinance itself... and therefore if it's better to fire normally from a longer range or get into range to beam aboard, yet avoid pesky point defence systems. I mean, by the end of the first Havenite war in the Honorverse capital ship missiles had ranges in excess of three light minutes, which, even if that universe had beaming technology would probably be outside it's range. Firecontrol of course sucked arse at that distance but throw enough birds in each broadside... The problem with a fleet train is that, in protecting it you have to pull ships from the front, stifiling your offensive capability. Either that or pull them from rear areas putting you at a defensive disadvantage against deep raids. Not to mention that rear-area ships would probably be older classes so you'd have to pull more of them to get the same effective defence. Hmm, I wonder what the endurance of a starfleet ship actually would be? Again, relateing back to the Honorverse, a Battlecruiser in the Royal Navy carries enough reactor mass for it's fusion plants to give it a cruising endurance of about 3 months, cruisers being the Navy's workhorses. Capital ships carry somewhat less (relatively for their size) as they can be resonably assured of regular re-supply so can give up bunkerage mass for other systems.

In Deep Space Nine, which I believe to be the finest Star Trek show ever made, the Federation discovered the Dominion, an enemy who had technology that was comparable, but superior in some very threatening ways. First their weapons were effective, even against shielded vessels. (This accounted for the destruction of the aforementioned Galaxy Class vessel, USS Odyssey.) Second, they could cast and manipulate Neutronium, the hardest substance known to Star Trek pseudo-science. (A feat the Federation never managed to duplicate.) And third, and most frighteningly, they could transport matter over light year distances! (Only without the need of a Stargate.) Unfortunately, the Dominion never fully exploited their obvious superiority in transporter technology. Primarily, it was used as a deus ex machina to allow Dominion characters a means of convenient escape/dramatic exit while vowing to fight another day. If they used their transporters to beam explosives over stellar distances through shielding onto Federation vessels, they may have prolonged the war another whole season. On the matter of Deep Space Nine in general. I think it was Star Trek's finest moment, not only because it starred Avery Brooks, the man who should be playing Destro in the upcoming G.I. Joe movie. In general, the show gave more thought than most to its tactical situation. The Dominion weren't the standard god-like alien menace who could only be defeated by an eleventh hour plot device, Or a half-hearted saber-rattler who could be talked of the precipice of conflict. They were a determined, invading force who had to be beaten back to their own territory through a lengthy campaign of vigilant tactics and brutal, bloody sacrifice. In Deep Space Nine, Starfleet won the peace that is so easily taken for granted in every other incarnation of Star Trek. What you mention about fleet trains here is brilliant! All the decisions you pointed out regarding the alocation and distribution of war material are precisely the problems that would add confront planners in an interstellar navy. There are plenty of stories to tell that grow organically from the fertile soil of a storie's own premise. It's a shame so few authors ever get their hands dirty.

Wow, that's a whole lot of beaming range. Man, how fast does a matter stream travel? Because over a light year that sounds like a long journey with the worst in-flight service ever... Hmm, I've not watched much Star Trek, but it sounds like Deep Space Nine definately needs to be added to the "urgent watch" list. I hear you on the "not using their technology" thing though. It makes it harder to believe that this is an all out struggle if both sides aren't using every option available. I think that's what I like about the Honor books too both sides are fighting tooth and nail and every action has consequences. For example, in order go face Haven, the Star Kingdom of Manticore had to ally itself with a number of, basically second rate governments in order to build up defensive depth and organise fleet bases along the envisaged war-front. However, at the end of the first Havenite war the Royal Navy's major offensive was delayed by a full 2 years as the self same allies clammored for reinforcements after a number of Havenite deep strikes, forcing the RMN to divert capital ships away from the buildup of 8th Fleet. That in turn slowed down the deployment of project Ghostrider (Manticore's next-gen weapons program) as the Admiralty, quite understandably, didn't want to reveal those capabilities untill the launch of said offensive. And then there's the political ramifications as both sides of the war don't want to risk pissing off the Solarian League, the largest political power in Honorverse. Think EU type bearaucracy with US levels of arrogance and it's a fair picture of the Solly outlook on the rest outlook of the universe. Haven or Manticore could eat them ship for ship but the Sollies have effectively unlimited manpower and could just keep throwing people and ships at them... ...and I'm rambling so shall shut up.

Well there are some points where science has to concede to brevity of narrative. As with subspace radio, Dominion transporters could transmit matter at hyperluminal speeds. This way, we are able to see a villain the very next episode after his last narrow escape from justice, as opposed to having him spend untold months in transit. I would very much recommend DS9, btw. I think of all the Star Trek spin-offs, it best explored the consequences of its conflicts. To cite an example, I recall a Next Generation episode in which the USS Enterprise caught a Romulan invasion force in Federation space. A warship and several troop transports were intercepted on their way to a Federation member world, and though they were initially operating under the guise of a peace envoy, events of the episode had opened the eyes of the Big E's crew to the true nature of the Romulan's Trojan horse. You'd think war was inevitable right? That, at this point the Romulan Star Empire would figure , "In for a penny, in for a pound" and try to fight their way to their objective; or that they'd attempt a withdrawal back to their territory, with their lumbering troop transports getting hammered by the Enterprise as it moves to ensure the evidence of Romulus' transgressions remain on the Federation side of the border. What happens instead? The Romulans destroy their own transports, killing tens of thousands of their own men to prevent being implicated in a preemptive invasion. Hogwash In Deep Space Nine the discovery of a stable wormhole leading to the distant territory of the Dominion leads to years of brutal bloody war for control of the passage way. In fact, the Federation looses control of the gateway for the first quarter of the sixth season. The battle to get it back, was probably one of the best space fights ever televised. Anyway, if you get a chance check out DS9. If it's any incentive, it shared many of the same writers as the modern Battlestar Galactica, and it's not without its share of similarities to the latter. Okay, now I'll stop rambling.
I just noticed the banks of torpedo tubes. Good gravy, this beast has one hell of a broadside! Klingon boys better run and hide....

I really should add this to my resolution drawings. It might be nice to do a modern picture of this beast, dishing it out to other vessels. I could see this ship actually being built, once the Dominion War got underway. Starfleet would need a vessel to offset the firepower of the Dominion battleship featured in the episode Valiant.

Yes, I can definitely see it in answer to the Dominion BB and the production Negh'vars. This is definitely a traditional lumbering first-rate ship-of-the-line that drops from warp and, one had better hope, does not cross one's T. A rippling broadside from such a ship must be *magnificent* to see. Fun fact: Many ships prior to the Washington Naval Treaty had broadside torpedo tubes. I doubt they'd be as impressive.
Broadside torpedo tubes. Wow. Makes one wounder what sort of monstrocities they would have wrought had no such treaty existed.

Well, the best example of what they would have done without the WNT is pretty much the Yamato-class dreadnought battleship. As trope-invoking as that class might be, 16.1-inch cannons not only violated the WNT and the London Amendments six ways to sunday, they definitely made Yamato and Musashi the Big Dogs of the seas, or at least they were intended to be.
Speaking of the Big "Y", have you seen the live action Space Battleship Yamato movie?

Not yet! Thanks for reminding me of it, I need to try and track it down. It looks... interesting. I can't say too much more than that without seeing it. (It was kind of hard to see any of the action going on in the trailers with the huge kanji in the way. ^_^ )
[link] This is how I got it. Also, there's a kiosk in the mall across from my gym that sells it along with Hong Kong cinema DVD's, but I doubt that would be of any help.

Excellent, thanks! And when I lived in NY, there was a kiosk just like that at the mall. I loved that little stand. You could find some of the most interesting movies that way. Warriors of Zu Mountain, Versus, Shaolin Soccer, Kung Fu Hustle... and on the Russian side of things, Nochnoy Dozor (Night Watch) (admittedly not as good as the English translation/dub.) Alas, the kiosk disappeared one day and became an RC model stand.
Russian cinema? It that a thing?

Sure is! Russian cinema has a flavor. Maybe... not a TASTY flavor, but a distinct flavor. Especially Russian fantasy/science fiction. *Especially* from the Soviet era. The original version of Solaris -- not the George Cloony version -- is a great example of later Soviet science fiction cinema. Not great by any means, but a good story. It's sometimes been called the 'Russian 2001' for really good reasons. Maybe give this one a pass unless you liked Cloony's Solaris. However, I cannot recommend highly enough the movie STALKER. The game is fairly loosely based on it, but it's a good film. Just the concepts alone are cool in it, and the cinematography is very well done. It's based on a Soviet-era story called 'Roadside Picnic' which itself is great. And there's the Night Watch trilogy -- Night Watch, Day Watch, and eventually Twilight Watch (though the rights to that last were bought by Fox, and heavens know if it'll ever come out.) Basically two armies of supernatural beings ages ago signed a ceasefire so they didn't end up destroying the world with their war. To ensure they each obeyed the treaty, they watch the actions of each other -- the Day Watch keeps tabs on the Light Others, and the Night Watch keeps tabs on the Dark Others. If you want to see it, find the English releases -- they're not dubbed, and they made the subtitles an integral part of the experience, such that they're almost a character unto themselves. They were made in 2004 on, and the special effects are pretty good. Somewhat related is the 2001 film Avalon, directed by Mamoru Oshii. (There's another film from 1998 called Avalon which is absolutely not the same.) It's not Russian, but Polish, but there's still some strong Soviet-era influence in the movie. I *really* recommend this one. Illegal virtual reality games, criminal conspiracies, military hardware, and some philosophy. Despite the kind of mind-bending third act (the guy directed both Ghost in the Shell movies, and it shows) it's got some cool concepts and effects. And the last scene is probably the most amazing -- if creepiest -- visual transformation of a character without using any special effects I've ever seen. ... Good lord, can I babble or what? Sorry; I went to animation school in 2002 and took visual effects and cinematography classes. They were right: It ruined watching films for me. ^_^;;
O.o I like it a lot. maybe it uses replicators to replicate many, many, MANY stuffies?

Photon Torpedo: Variable yield matter/antimatter warhead. Tried and true super-lumenal combat weapon. Quantum Torpedo: Variable yield artificial quantum singularity warhead. Also nicknamed "Implosion Bomb," and "Romulan Party Favor," owing to its origins on the other side of the Neutral Zone. ) Stuffies: Standard torpedo casings,filled with tribbles held in stasis, along with enough synthetic grain to sustain a 1000 fold multiplication upon resuscitation! Rumors of the mere existence of this resource annihilating weapon has kept the Klingon Empire in check for more than a century.

Honestly at first I was like, oh another Starfleet ship as since the ST:TNG aired there've been a boatload of them designed. And while no less impressive than any other as far as art quality goes, it was just another ship at this point. It's like getting another design on a lightsaber at this point. But then I read your description of the history of this and now this is just one of the baddest ass ships the Fleet will ever see haha!

Yes, thanks be to DS9 and its wealth of fleet battles for the diversity that we would eventually experience in starship design. Losing a sense of uniqueness and novelty was a small price to pay to know that the real custodians of Star Trek's rights were working hard to expand the canon. (Or the cannon, as the case may be.) Be nice if Galactica followed suit. [link]
True, though it's tough to build anything new other than the Vipers when you're on the run from Cylons.

I mean before the attack. Something as big an complex as a battlestar, I imagine would need to operate more that a mere two kinds of space vehicle to see to its mission/logistic/ecm needs. I concede that the harsh realities of television production preclude depicting the full diversity of craft one would see on say, a Nimitz class supercarrier. (Which would have 1/4 the operational capacity of a Galactica type battlestar, if they existed.) But my mechaphile imagination can't help wanting to see more heavy metal in my science fiction shows. Actually, now that I think of it, even after the fall of the colonies, it would be nice to see jury-rigged, two engined vipers, or reverse-engineered captured raiders, or more home grown vehicles like the "Laura" pressed into service for the increasingly desperate colonials. Oh well, BSG's just about wrapped up anyway. Maybe they'll be another show about fighter planes in space one day.

Well they have the TV movie (which I'm sure won't suddenly catapult the production design team back to the drawing board) but then afterwards there's the prequel show Caprica. So maybe we will see different stuff on that show.

Thanks for holding out hope, but I don't know if they're going to do much with the colonial military with that show. The brief preview I saw looked like Ronald D Moore was getting every salacious thing they wouldn't let him do on Star Trek out of his system, particularly concerning the holodecks. If the teaser is any indication, it seems that RDM is going to try and explore the civilian life, which is often neglected in speculative fiction, particularly when it's televised. But admittedly, I did only see a teaser. Perhaps there's more to it. Anythings possible at this point.

And if the federation didn't buy then there is always the other ship toting massive firepower factions out there... they would simply have to get past you a lowly human or adorian, blue skin guys little atennae that that your prof-sig depicts possibly

"they would simply have to get past you a lowly human or adorian, blue skin guys little atennae that that your prof-sig depicts possibly." Run that by me again? I'm having trouble following what you're trying to say.

First Sorry and on closer inspection I recognize your profile image as Destro from the original [link] cartoon but back to what I posted... amazing how 15+ years of last seeing the original tends to make one not recognize them. And in retrospect I hate it when the keyboard doesn't register a keystroke. Most races in Star Trek when first encounter humans tend to view them as a weak inferior species or some other reason, Klingons we have no Honor, and except for Vulcans who saw us as emotionally grounded in our ways... I meant the Andorians from the ST Enterprise which I have been watching on Sci-fi, my only fix currently, are the ones Captain Archer encounters and has recurring meetings with throughout the series is who I intially thought of... why I don't know. Still excellently drawn and I could see this as the Federations attempt to balance things out after the Dominion War and the Delta Quadrant i.e. the Borg. Also, the ship could have Torpedo launchers that say could be adjusted to fit non-federation issue torpedos i.e. Klingon, Romulan, Cardassian, etcetera that are salvaged from the battlefield of the not completely destroyed ships and looks to have a flight deck on the back between the nacelles

Ah, that clears things up. Thanks. You know I was just thinking about Jeffrey Combs, whom I believe played the Andorian that Captain Archer had the obligatory duel to the death with. In DS9, the same actor played a member of the Dominion, who in Captain Sisko's presence, looked like a good stiff wind could knock him over. I'm guessing that means Avery Brooks is a good head taller than Scott Bakula This says nothing of the respective command qualities of the characters each man portrayed, especially when you consider that when Enterprise finally got its act together in its fourth season, it began to define Archer as the inspiration reason why two centuries later Sisko would say, "Men don't join Starfleet to be commanders. Or admirals." But it does say which captain you want at your back in a bar fight.

Wow- You did a fantastic job selling the concept: The ship on TNG kept getting disabled by things like thousands-year old booby-traps, or even the library that they found drifting out in the middle of nowhere for fifty thousand years that gave Data like twelve personalities. I'm not sure if a warship would fare any better against one of the countless subspace anomalies floating out there: It would seem that there's Federation vessels being gulped down on a weekly basis. Of course, there was more of that in Voyager than in any other series, as I recall, so maybe they should just stay the hell out of the delta quadrant...
Oh man, don't get me started on Voyager. That show was flawed coming out of the gate, and unlike Enterprise, which found a direction in its final year on the air, Voyager never shaped up.
This is really good!
Thank you.
Behemoth is right..that thing is intimidating! awesome design. I wouldn't mind taking a crack at modeling that.
That would be absolutely awesomesauce! Let me know if you'd need any thing from my end.

I read an earlier post of yours that said you would change a few things if you had to do it over again..like windows and such. If I decide to tackle this, I'll base it from that standpoint. I'm working on a Daedalus Class ship right now, Maybe I'll go after your behemoth next.

Awesome work. I really like this ships design especially the integration of the warp nacelle into the saucer. However, in regards to the comment on the bottom about making a ton of money if there was money in the Star Trek Universe. That is my big problem with Star Trek. It's full of Socialism. That was why I preferred Alternate Universe presented in the Enterprise episode "A Mirror Darkly" where humans killed the Vulcans during First Contact and jacked them for their ship and created an Empire. Now that is how the Federation should roll.

Heh, while "In a Mirror Darkly" was by far the best episode of Enterprise, I think that outright barbarism and piracy is too far on the other side of the pendulum for my taste. I think the best portrayal of a future democracy could be found in Babylon 5. But then, B5 had the best of many things. Reboot Galactica started out looking to be as good, but then it stopped making any damn sense.

Yeah, Galactica lost me when they blew up the Pegasus and tried colonizing that planet that got invaded by the Cylons at the end of the second season. Then they really lost me when they started getting really religious. By the way, have you seen Firefly? That was a great series too.

Yea, Firefly was beyond excellent. Shame it didn't last very long. Did you read the most recent Dark Horse "Serenity" comic? It's the only place now where we can get Firefly stories, and the most recent one-shot "Float-Out" had the best ending possible.

Yeah, I gotta confess that I did not give it a look until after it was off the air. I was channel surfing once and paused long enough to see them riding in a horse drawn wagon that looked too western for me and thought that they were doing some Stark Trek holodeck crap or time warp and never gave it a second look. Regretfully, I did not learn that there was a reason for the western look since they were often flying to planets are the far rim of human colonized worlds... like the old west where people were in search of gold and a new way of life. I need to check out those comics too.
that is AWESOME!!!!
Thanks for the kind words.
your very welcome!!! keep it up!!!
IIIIIIIIII like it!!!!
Awesome! Glad to hear it. Or rather, read it.
No prob! XD

this looks awesome and you fleshed out the idea well. but you have one problem with your description, the nacelle does not create the warp power but merely creates the field around the ship, thats what the warp core is for
hes right
Interesting point. I suppose that what I get for taking my ideas from Franz Joseph. Well, for future reference I can simply say that the saucer section has its own independent warp core.
that works

I only have one "issue", and it's with your description. Where was it established that the Melbourne was Galaxy class? I recall it being seen on-screen as Excelsior in "The Best of Both Worlds" (the ship Admiral Hansen arrived on). She was the one in the tractor beam that got blown up immediately in the opening of DS9's "Emissary". [link]

You are right. It was never solidly established that the Melborne was an Excelsior class vessel. That's a definite oversight on my part. However, a Galaxy class vessel was destroyed at Wolf 359, The same site you referenced bears that out, at least. It also identifies this unnamed Galaxy class vessel as Hanson's ship, since the same bridge set was used to film his last communication. I had always assumed that the Excelsior class vessel that delivered Hansen and Shelby was the USS Hood. Is there any place where the identity of that ship is solidly established?

Actually, sorry. I was saying that it WAS established that the Melbourne was Excelsior class and not a Galaxy class. The picture on Memory Alpha is from the opening of "Emissary", the pilot of Deep Space Nine, which shows the battle actually taking place. The first ship destroyed is the Melbourne, and you can actually see the name on the hull if you look close enough, just before the Borg cutting beam blows the primary hull apart.

I'm not debating the fact that it was the Melborne at Wolf 359 or on the Emissary. It makes good sense from a narrative standpoint that a vessel mentioned in TNG would be showcased in the opening moments of DS9--bridging the two shows with a familiar element. I'm sorry if was unclear in my previous post that I agreed with this point, given the information provided. I was saying that while I was in error regarding the identity of the vessel, a Galaxy class ship was still lost at Wolf. I believe its remains can be seen in the upper right section of the view screen when the Enterprise encounters the battle's aftermath. I'm still not convinced it was the Melborne that dropped Hansen and Shelby off on Jouret Four. Hansen mentioned that the Melborne was awaiting a new captain. I can see a ship without a commanding officer being pressed into service once the Borg crisis was solidly established, but before then, I don't see it being used to ferry a flag officer and his aide. Without new information, I inclined to assume it was the USS Hood--the ship they usually show in that particular clip of library footage.

title: Silent Mobius Kiddy
description: Folks who know me, know that I have only the most fleeting tolerance for Magic, Ghosts, Ghouls, The Occult, Vampires, or any other of the metaphysical, supernatural claptrap that establishes its own flimsy set of rules before immediately proceeding to heedlessly ignore them.* However, if the aforementioned claptrap is put before a Syd Mead-esque skyline, dotted by crushing multitudes of luminous, crystal-faceted sky scrapers, and swarmed by levitating traffic jams of flying cars and bill-board zeppelins**--I might give it a second look. Add a team of hot anime babes to savagely hunt down and exterminate said supernatural elements with an ever growing arsenal of high tech weapons; and I'll probably watch the whole thing. I may even enjoy myself. Such was the case with the venerable Silent Möbius. After all, who here will debate the lower-case divinity of manga artist Kia Asamiya? I dare say the adventures of Kiddy Phenil and the agrammatically named Attacked Mystification Police almost makes up for there never having been a Bubblegum Crisis manga. *I much prefer Science Fiction; which establishes its own rules based on provable scientific principles, right before immediately proceeding to heedlessly ignore them. **In other words the "Not-too-distant-cyberpunk-dystopian-future!" My second favorite fictional backdrop.

comments.body: Well with such a glowing review, I may give it a look see. Personally I prefer Steampunk universes which set up science and heedlessly ignore them.

Hmmm, Steampunk, really seems to have come in vogue of late. In a way it reminds me of the Flinstones, which was , at its heart a comedic tale about 1950's America told with stone-age analogues to the period conveniences. Steampunk does the same thing with its mechanical computers, automobiles with boiler engines, and submarines with exposed, ornamental rivets. Hmmm, were the Flintstones created now, would it be called Rockpunk?

It might be, yeah. I'm not sure how "in vogue" Steampunk is, to be honest. I've been a fan of it since I can remember. Not really... Steampunk, but more Clockwork / Pre-digital technology. Tech as old as civilization itself and on up through to about the late 1940's. It all appeals to me, so while I use the term "Steampunk" as a catch-all... I'm not exactly really Steampunk. Just a fan of antique tech.
And of course, any excuse for a policewoman uniform is fine too.
Particularly one's with short skirts. On a slightly related note, I pledge my allegiance to Fullmetal Alchemist's Roy Mustang's ultimate goal to shrink the hemlines among all his female troops!
He's a hero and an example to all uniform regulations everywhere.

Absolutely marvellous work ^^ Love the policewoman, and her sexy slightly short very figure hugging uniform And her vehicle is AWESOME, has a magnificent "doesn't exist, but so easily could" sort of feel to it
Bet it steers like a cow.... And... "Phallic much?" *grin*
Still looks cool. lol I suppose *shrugs and rolls eyes* personally I've always just liked the long sweeping, graceful but powerful, carlines you see in cartoons.
Well I have to give credit to Asamiya's awesome design's there. I just tried to do them some semblance of justice.
Well I think you certainly succeeded there
I don't know which is hotter from that series, the ladies or the vehicles.
The ladies. But the vehicles are a close second.
Awesome work.
Thanks.
Your welcome dude. Keep producing amazing art.
never heard of Silent Mobius O-o but that girl looks unerringly like Major Motoko Kusanagi (Ghost in the Shell. which is BORING. nice erts though)

While it also has hot chick super cops in the not too distant dystopian cyberpunk future, Kia Asamia's Silent Mobius was another animal altogether, featuring multiple heroines, and pitting them against supernatural adversaries as opposed to the sci-fi/sociopolitical antagonists of GiTS. As for GiTS being boring, it depends on which incarnation you saw. The first two movies, while being beautiful and lushly animated, were glacially paced, and often wallowed in didactic and pretentious, navel-gazing monologues. This is radically corrected by the faster paced television series Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex. The expanded format of episodic television allowed the creators to ration out the philosophical points they wanted to make, while incorporating enough action to keep you excited and enough character to make you care. Plus the main heroine doesn't mind meld with a evil AI hacker. Forget about the movies, watch the GiTS show. If you think that's boring, there's no help for you.

I don't even know if I've seen the movies. I read the manga... which btw what is with that scene where all these girls are on a fake vacation? Like... am I interpreting things wrong or was it strongly implied that they were all FUCKING? And I've seen a few scattered eps of Ghost in the Shell on Adult Swim. My problem with both is the very thing you mentioned: they dictate during what is supposed to be a animated story. In stories your characters and the plot should show/argue for your philosophy. Not a bunch of cute little machine bug thingies together debating for 20 minutes. I would also like to point out that they didn't take their audience members into consideration enough, since media people generally consider the public to have the attention span of five year olds. Obviously this isn't true though, since GITS has done so well. Plus, since it seems to be beloved of both anime fans and comic geeks (Speak of the devil *waves*), people would have probably bitched like crazy if they "dumbed down" the character's lines. Why the hell did I write all this? DX geez. I sound like the lamest most stuck up geek ever.
What did you ink this with? The lines are so bold and smooth, I can't figure out if it's pen or brush. Anyway, nice work. It has a clean, old-school look that you don't see much anymore.
Pencil, with the contrast turned up. Yea I do missed the more hard edged look of golden age anime. It's high time for a come back.
so I take from the second paragaph that you like BladeRunner
Naturally.
as do we all

title: DMV a' la FLCL Black n White
description: A black and white rendering of my shameless Sadamoto FLCL composition swipe [link] only this time done in black and white. I know that 90% of people prefer a color image in all instances to black and white art, but I am much more satisfied with how this piece turned out than its color counterpart. The digital painting was more of an experiment at the time, and I felt I lost a little control of the underlying drawing with the new medium. This one I feel shows more confidence. Next time: Same thing--only chibi.

comments.body: "Thug Zapper"! Bwahahaha!

Finally someone noticed that. It's a shotgun-like weapon that fires the artificial equivalent of Texas Ball Lightning at a target. Sure I could also have called it a "Wireless Taser," but that just wasn't as catchy.
Well, you know me, I think. I can't resist a pun.

title: Autumn Silk Screen
description: A rejected design for a t-shirt to go with the series of pictures that Autumn Walk was a part of. In addition to the conspicuous lack of mecha, this drawing was to be printed in silk screen; a process so frustrating and restrictive that it is my hope that it has since been rendered utterly obsolete by modern printing processes, and those laboring under its yoke, eventually liberated. Case in point: I was limited to seven colors for this image: Red, White, Blue, Black, Blue, Light-Flesh and Yellow-Orange. After the fun I had with the published images, this was shaping up to be an enormous pain by comparison. But once again, I actually had fun puzzling out how to arrange a stark palette into an interesting composition, Sadly, the client didn't like how I cropped off the character's legs in order to create a pinwheel shape to the image. Now that I look at it, it does remind one of a legless Japanese spirit, but I'm still more or less satisfied with how things turned out.

comments.body: wow, very nice.
Thank you very much. I honestly wasn't expecting a "wow".

Didn't crop the legs? Your client has nary any imagination. It looks better without it, especially without a background. And no, the color of the prospective T-shirt that it's going to be printed on does not count as "background".
Perhaps not, but it does help the imagination impaired client to visualize how the image will look printed on a shirt.
I love such old school faces

I wish I could say I was clever and "retro", but I'm just old, and this is just the "facial typography" that was in vogue when I was a fan boy. I suppose I could try to emulate the newer stuff, but I find something flat and soulless about it.
This looks great ~! Really love the kimono design ^ ^
Thank you.

title: Bubble Alternate
description: It's amazing what you find when you're looking for something else. Here is rejected composition for the Bubblegum Girls poster. [link] Looking at it again I think I prefer this composition. The poses of the girls are more varied, plus you get a better look at Linna's butt. In addition, the formation the Knight Sabres are in suggests an aerial shot of Sylia's hq/railgun. I think I'm going to have to finish this.

comments.body: Seconded on the finishing!
Well now that I have a computer that doesn't take 15 minutes to save an image, (Gawd, how did I put up with that? ) I might just take you up on that.

We put up because we have to. When we don't? Paaaartay! Though I admit that I'd love to see the effort go to some Empowered superchica fan art first. I'm still amazed you haven't tackled Spookums yet. >.>
Hmmm.
finish eeeet
Haw, you're like a voice in my head.
do I also make you burn things? heh.
Naw that's your head demon. Mine won't shut up about strawberry cheesecake icecream. It'll be the end of me one day, I know it.
Awesome!!! Are commissions still undergoing?
'Fraid so.
's okay! I will wait!! As usual your drawings are awesome (and I LOVE BGC!!!)
Linna has a nice ass.
Main reason why I preferred this composition.
Are you going to color it?
Count on it.
Seriously? I love your marker work and I would love to see you finish it. I definatly would buy a poster of either one of these versions! :3
Thanks you. That's very reassuring to hear.

title: The Good Sister
description: Another look at Sabrina the GELF* pilot for America's front line Giant Space Robot forces. Her mech is visible amidst the overwrought line work, as I illustrated this during my "Too much is never enough" inking phase. I still find the picture altogether salvageable. I may even color it not that I've excavated to old line art. Who knows? *Genetically Engineered Life Form.

comments.body: Very Very impressive work mate ^^ You familiar at all with the Battle Sisters, aka the Adepta Sororitas, of Warhammer 40K?

Well, there are no religious affiliations with my GELF sisters. Quite the opposite in fact.
